3.name: Temple Form
3.html:
You have developed a signature combat form — a kata system specific to your body's enhancement configuration. Choose one Temple Form: - Water Form: Your movement does not provoke opportunity attacks, and you can move through enemy spaces (but not end your turn there) freely. - Fire Form: When you deal damage with a melee attack, the target cannot take reactions until the start of your next turn. - Iron Form: When you take damage, you can use your reaction to halve it and immediately make one melee attack against the creature that damaged you.

You can switch between forms during a short rest.

6.name: Spiritual Violence
6.html: Your conviction that combat is sacred has a tangible effect. Your attack cards deal an additional 3 resonance damage (fixed). Additionally, when you drop a creature to 0 HP, you regain HP equal to your RESONANCE (minimum 1). This represents the spiritual completion of the act.

10.name: Perfect Instrument
10.html: Your body is a temple. It has become extraordinarily resilient. Your HP maximum increases by 20 (retroactive to all prior levels). Additionally, you gain +3 to FRAME-based resistance checks, and when you make a death resistance check, you can add your RESONANCE to the result.

14.name: The Temple Burns
14.html:
At the deepest level of the Flesh Temple philosophy is an acceptance of destruction as transformation. Once per long rest, you can enter the Temple Burns state as a bonus action. For 1 minute: - You deal maximum damage on all melee attacks. - You have resistance to all damage types. - At the end of the minute, you take 22 resonant damage that cannot be reduced by any means. This is the offering. You accept it as part of the practice.

---

system.description: These samurai have taken the most internally demanding interpretation of the Iron Sutra: the body itself, properly enhanced and properly trained, is the temple. Combat is not a profession or an obligation — it is a form of worship. Every strike is a prayer. Every wound received is an offering. They maintain exquisite physical discipline not for military utility but for spiritual completeness. They are often called the most dangerous samurai in a fight and the most difficult samurai to serve alongside — their detachment from conventional military objectives can make them unreliable, but their absolute commitment to combat-as-practice makes them devastating.
